title: The Joys of Compounding
subtitle: The Passionate Pursuit of Lifelong Learning
author: Gautam Baid
authorBio: Gautam Baid, CFA, is the Managing Partner of Stellar Wealth Partners India Fund (Delaware-based, available to US accredited investors) and Equity Advisor of Stellar Wealth PMS (available to Indian citizens and NRIs). Both funds model their fee structure after the original Buffett Partnership. Previously Portfolio Manager at Summit Global Investments, an SEC-registered investment advisor in Salt Lake City, USA. Before that, Senior Analyst at Citigroup and Deutsche Bank investment banking teams across Mumbai, London, and Hong Kong. CFA charterholder and CFA Institute member. Profiled in Morningstar's Learn from the Masters series in 2018 and 2019. Author of two books: The Joys of Compounding (2020–2021) and The Making of a Value Investor (2022), the latter chronicling his investment evolution during the Indian bear market of 2018–2020. Website: thejoysofcompounding.com
keyConcepts: Compounding as a universal principle — applies to knowledge, relationships, health, and goodwill, not just money
Lifelong learning as the highest-return investment available to any human being
Mental models / latticework of mental models (Munger) — big ideas from major disciplines, applied in combination
Behavioral edge over informational edge — the source of sustainable investor alpha in the internet age
Hyperbolic discounting — the behavioral bias that chronically undervalues long-horizon compounding businesses
Simplicity as the highest form of intellect (Einstein's five-level framework)
Minimalism as an extension of simplicity — removing the unnecessary from both investing and life
Inversion as primary decision tool — four-question framework for stock evaluation
Circle of competence — honesty about what you can and cannot evaluate
Return of capital before return on capital — capital preservation precedes compounding
Prudent diversification as defense against the unknown (20–25 stocks across industries)
Financial independence as prerequisite for patient, non-urgent investing
Value traps: cyclicality illusion, app/disruption risk, capital misallocation, governance failure
Investment journal — the $10 notebook as one of the highest-ROI investing tools
Funnel not sponge — goodwill compounds when knowledge is shared
First-principles thinking applied to business and life
notableQuotes: Today, after having successfully achieved financial freedom through my passionate pursuit of lifelong learning, I can happily say that I am a better investor because I am a lifelong learner, and I am a better lifelong learner because I am an investor.
The best thing a human being can do is to help another human being know more.
Reading is also meditative and calming. Books are truly life-changing.
Simplicity is the end result of long, hard work, not the starting point.
Fifty years ago, the best investors were the ones with an informational edge. Today, the best investors are the ones with a behavioral edge.
The winners also lose occasionally, but those who help others win can never lose.
Our goodwill compounds when we share with others. We should act as a funnel, not a sponge.
Minimalism is basically an extension of simplicity — not only do you take things from complex to simple but also you try to get rid of anything that is unnecessary.
Invest in yourself. The more you learn, the more you earn. An investment in knowledge pays the best interest.
A long and healthy life is the key to compounding so eat less junk food and sugar, get sufficient sleep and engage in regular moderate exercise.
There is no alternative to hard work.
Any stock that has compounded at 15 percent to 20 percent for decades was, by definition, undervalued by the market for long periods of time.
Always consider the outside view which takes base rates and statistics into account rather than only the inside view which makes predictions based on a narrow set of inputs.
The frequency of correctness does not matter; it is the magnitude of correctness that matters.
warrenBuffettQuote: Gautam's work beautifully captures the essence of value investing as a discipline that extends far beyond the markets — it is a way of thinking about life itself.
readability: Accessible; conversational prose with practical examples; no finance background required; suitable for investors and non-investors alike
